title: Bessel van der Kolk's The Body Keeps the Score: Key Takeaways
excerpt: Explore key insights from Bessel van der Kolk's The Body Keeps the Score on trauma’s impact and somatic healing.
body:
Introduction: Why The Body Keeps the Score Matters

Bessel van der Kolk’s The Body Keeps the Score is a foundational text in trauma research and somatic healing. Drawing on decades of clinical experience and neuroscience, van der Kolk explains how trauma reshapes the brain and body, making traditional talk therapy often insufficient for healing. This article breaks down key takeaways from the book, providing a clear, science-grounded understanding of trauma's impact and practical insights for self-directed somatic healing.

1. Trauma is Stored in the Body, Not Just the Mind

Van der Kolk emphasizes that trauma is not only a psychological event but a physiological one. The nervous system encodes traumatic experiences in the body’s tissues and nervous pathways, leading to chronic activation or shutdown. This explains why survivors often feel “stuck” in their bodies with symptoms like hypervigilance, dissociation, or somatic pain—even when they intellectually understand their trauma.

Research supports this view. Peter Levine, in his work on Somatic Experiencing, highlights how trauma disrupts the body’s natural rhythm of arousal and release, causing freezing or fight/flight states to become chronic (Levine, 1997). Similarly, Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the autonomic nervous system’s regulation is compromised in trauma, preventing safe social engagement and self-soothing (Porges, 2011).

What this means for healing

Healing trauma requires interventions that address the body’s stored sensations and nervous system dysregulation, not just cognitive processing. This insight opens the door to somatic therapies, mindfulness, movement, breathwork, and other body-based practices.

2. The Brain Is Rewired by Trauma

Van der Kolk details how trauma changes the brain’s structure and function. Key areas affected include the amygdala (threat detection), hippocampus (memory integration), and prefrontal cortex (executive function and regulation). These changes cause survivors to become hyper-alert to danger, have fragmented memories, and struggle with emotional regulation.

Neuroscientific studies confirm these findings. For example, functional MRI scans show increased amygdala activity and reduced connectivity with regulatory prefrontal areas in people with PTSD (Shin et al., 2006). This neuroplasticity means the brain can be rewired—but it requires approaches that engage both body and mind.

3. Talk Therapy Alone Often Isn’t Enough

While talk therapy is essential, van der Kolk argues that it often falls short because it doesn’t fully address the body and nervous system’s role in trauma. Survivors may get stuck reliving traumatic memories without resolution, or feel retraumatized by focusing only on narrative.

He advocates for integrative treatments that combine cognitive approaches with somatic regulation techniques. This includes Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR), yoga, neurofeedback, and sensory-based therapies.

EMDR as an Example

Francine Shapiro’s EMDR uses bilateral stimulation to help the brain reprocess traumatic memories, reducing their emotional charge (Shapiro, 2018). Van der Kolk reports strong clinical success with EMDR, highlighting its unique ability to engage brain-body integration.

4. The Importance of Safe Relationships and Social Engagement

Drawing from Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory, van der Kolk emphasizes that trauma disrupts our capacity for safe social connection. Trauma survivors often experience isolation, mistrust, and difficulty regulating emotions within relationships.

Social engagement activates the ventral vagal complex, promoting safety and regulation. Healing requires rebuilding trust and connection through attuned relationships, group therapies, or community support (Dana, 2018).

5. Somatic Practices Help Regulate the Nervous System

Van der Kolk champions somatic interventions that help trauma survivors regain nervous system regulation. This includes:

  • Yoga and Movement: Gentle, mindful movement helps survivors reconnect with their bodies and release tension.
  • Breathwork: Controlled breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting calm.
  • Mindfulness and Body Awareness: Techniques that cultivate present-moment awareness reduce dissociation and hypervigilance.
  • Somatic Experiencing: Peter Levine’s approach focuses on tracking bodily sensations to complete incomplete defensive responses.

These practices help restore the natural rhythm of arousal and relaxation that trauma disrupts.

6. Trauma Affects Development and Interpersonal Patterns

Van der Kolk explores how early childhood trauma alters attachment patterns and brain development, leading to difficulties with emotional regulation, self-worth, and relationships in adulthood. This aligns with Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy framework, which addresses how trauma shapes body schemas and relational patterns from a somatic perspective (Ogden, 2015).

Complex trauma requires addressing these developmental wounds through body-based therapies that integrate safety, regulation, and relational repair.

7. The Role of Narrative and Meaning-Making

While somatic healing is crucial, van der Kolk doesn’t dismiss the power of narrative. Integrating the trauma story in a coherent way supports healing by creating meaning and reducing fragmentation. Dan Siegel’s concept of "mindsight"—the ability to perceive one’s own mind and that of others—helps survivors develop integration between emotional, cognitive, and somatic experiences (Siegel, 2010).

8. Trauma Healing Is Possible with a Multimodal Approach

Van der Kolk’s core message is hopeful: trauma survivors can heal by engaging the whole self—body, brain, and relationships. No single approach works for everyone, but a combination of somatic, cognitive, relational, and neurobiological interventions offers the best chance for recovery.

“Trauma results in a fundamental reorganization of the way mind and brain manage perceptions.” — Bessel van der Kolk

His work has transformed trauma treatment by validating the body’s role and opening pathways for innovative therapies.

Conclusion: Applying Van der Kolk’s Insights to Your Healing

If you’ve tried therapy and still feel stuck, understanding how trauma lives in your body and nervous system can shift your healing path. Explore somatic practices, seek safe relational connections, and consider integrative therapies that engage your whole nervous system. Van der Kolk’s research invites a compassionate, science-backed approach to reclaiming safety and vitality.

For further learning, explore foundational authors like Peter Levine, Stephen Porges, Deb Dana, Pat Ogden, and Francine Shapiro who expand on these principles. Your body truly does keep the score—but it can also be the gateway to profound healing.

References

  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton.
  • Levine, P. (1997). Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma. North Atlantic Books.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2015). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. Norton.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Norton.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2010). Mindsight: The New Science of Personal Transformation. Bantam.
  • Shin, L. M., Rauch, S. L., & Pitman, R. K. (2006). Amygdala, Medial Prefrontal Cortex, and Hippocampal Function in PTSD. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 1071(1), 67-79.

faqs.q: Why does trauma affect the body as well as the mind?
faqs.a: Trauma triggers nervous system responses that become stored as physical sensations and patterns, not just mental memories. This is why survivors often feel bodily symptoms like tension, pain, or dissociation.

faqs.q: Can talk therapy alone heal trauma?
faqs.a: While talk therapy helps process trauma cognitively, it often misses the nervous system and bodily components, which are essential for full healing. Integrating somatic therapies improves outcomes.

faqs.q: What role does the nervous system play in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: The nervous system regulates our stress responses; trauma can dysregulate it, causing chronic hyperarousal or shutdown. Healing involves restoring nervous system regulation through body-based practices.

faqs.q: How can I start somatic healing on my own?
faqs.a: Begin with gentle body awareness practices like mindful breathing, grounding exercises, or yoga. These help you reconnect with your body safely and regulate nervous system arousal.
